title: Phantoms in the Brain: Probing the Mysteries of the Human Mind
author: V. S. Ramachandran, Sandra Blakeslee
themes: Phantom Limbs and the Body Image
excerpt: V. S. Ramachandran, one of the most imaginative neuroscientists of our time, uses bizarre neurological case studies—phantom limbs, Capgras syndrome, out-of-body experiences—to explore the deepest questions about consciousness, identity, and the nature of the self.
keyIdeas: Phantom limbs reveal that the body image is a construct of the brain
Mirror neurons may explain empathy, imitation, and the evolution of culture
The brain is a collection of modules that can malfunction independently
Neurological syndromes illuminate the normal functioning of the mind
keyTakeaways: The sense of having a body is created by the brain and can be fooled
Unusual neurological conditions provide windows into normal brain function
The self is not a unified entity but a coalition of competing modules
Simple experiments can reveal profound truths about the mind
whoShouldRead: Anyone fascinated by the strangeness of the human mind
Students of neuroscience, psychology, and philosophy of mind
Readers who enjoyed Oliver Sacks and want more depth
Anyone interested in consciousness and the self
whoShouldSkip: Readers uncomfortable with discussions of brain damage and disability
Those looking for mainstream, consensus neuroscience
